metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
It may be taken as a fundamental axiom that a colored object will not appear the same, in general, under two illuminants differing in spectral character. A red fabric appears red under daylight or under a tungsten lamp because it has the ability to reflect the red rays and to absorb all or nearly all of the other colored rays. But the color of the red fabric depends upon the illuminant because, [masked clause describing a cause or reason].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: for example, it will appear black or a brownish-black under an ordinary mercury arc
for example, it will appear blue or green under an ordinary mercury arc
for example, the arcs are in use for blueprinting and photo-engraving and many tungsten lamps of all sizes are in use for printing

the color-sensation in the eye is always the integral result of all the individual color-sensations which may be due to the radiant energy of the various wave-lengths present in the illuminant or reflected or transmitted by colored media
the illuminant determines which wavelengths are reflected and absorbed
because the spectral composition of illumination that selects the wavelengths reflected by the fabric
passage: It may be taken as a fundamental axiom that a colored object will not appear the same, in general, under two illuminants differing in spectral character. A red fabric appears red under daylight or under a tungsten lamp because it has the ability to reflect the red rays and to absorb all or nearly all of the other colored rays. But the color of the red fabric depends upon the illuminant because, for example, it will appear black or a brownish-black under an ordinary mercury arc.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
Few working rules are available which may be depended upon at the present time for designing lighting installations from this broader view which aims to do justice to the potentiality of light and, therefore, the lighting specialist must draw upon various sources of information and through imagination, experiment, and experience he should be able to utilize light in lighting effects in a manner similar to that of the painter whose media are pigments. A great amount of lighting may be done very well without resorting to computations. In fact much of the present practice is the result of experience and judgment in which computations are practically impossible or unnecessary. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation] By choosing the proper lighting equipment the desired general effect may be obtained and by computation the required luminous output of each unit may be determined.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: However, in many cases it is necessary to have a certain illumination intensity on the floor or on an imaginary plane somewhat above it.
However, in many cases it is unnecessary to have a certain illumination intensity on the ceiling or on an imaginary plane somewhat below it.

However, it is impossible to compute the amount of light contributed to a certain point by reflections from various surfaces in a room so that computations must be based upon the general results of experiment.
But in practising lighting from the engineer's viewpoint dominantly and persistently, the results are naturally those of illuminating engineering.
However, the true art of lighting design lies not merely in calculations but also in the creative vision.

Nevertheless, designers should not assume that meticulous calculations are indispensable, for intuition and experience frequently yield effective lighting outcomes without exhaustive computation in practice.
passage: Few working rules are available which may be depended upon at the present time for designing lighting installations from this broader view which aims to do justice to the potentiality of light and, therefore, the lighting specialist must draw upon various sources of information and through imagination, experiment, and experience he should be able to utilize light in lighting effects in a manner similar to that of the painter whose media are pigments. A great amount of lighting may be done very well without resorting to computations. In fact much of the present practice is the result of experience and judgment in which computations are practically impossible or unnecessary. However, in many cases it is necessary to have a certain illumination intensity on the floor or on an imaginary plane somewhat above it. By choosing the proper lighting equipment the desired general effect may be obtained and by computation the required luminous output of each unit may be determined.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
The foregoing illustrates that decoration is intimately interwoven with lighting in the broader sense. If we are to employ light to the limit of its possibilities in lighting effects, the texture and the distribution of brightness and color of the surroundings must be considered. The mood or impression of an interior may be modified by varying the distribution and color of the light even if the decorations remain fixed; [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. At this point the harmony and esthetics of color enter the lighting problem very prominently but it is beyond the scope of this volume to discuss these intimately.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: however, this is a handicap in lighting which could be removed by considering lighting and decoration jointly
however, this is an advantage in lighting which could be enhanced by considering lighting and decoration jointly
but before the lighting specialist is able to utilize the scientific possibilities and the charm of color to their fullest extent, he must be intimately acquainted with light itself
but doubtless lighting units will be developed to meet the needs discussed in the foregoing paragraphs
however,  the lighting itself can change perception of color
however, the subtle interplay of shadows can transform perception more dramatically than any ornament, without altering decor
passage: The foregoing illustrates that decoration is intimately interwoven with lighting in the broader sense. If we are to employ light to the limit of its possibilities in lighting effects, the texture and the distribution of brightness and color of the surroundings must be considered. The mood or impression of an interior may be modified by varying the distribution and color of the light even if the decorations remain fixed; however, this is a handicap in lighting which could be removed by considering lighting and decoration jointly. At this point the harmony and esthetics of color enter the lighting problem very prominently but it is beyond the scope of this volume to discuss these intimately.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
White naturally assumed the attributes of chastity, virginity, purity, innocence, fidelity, peace and friendship; and mythology and poetry abound with examples of such usage. It also symbolizes weakness perhaps through association with femininity and was the mark of untried manhood. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect] Black naturally is quite opposite in character to white, being characterized as dismal, desolate, deadly, malignant, threatening, evil, chaotic and loathesome.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: Consistently therefore it is appropriately the flag of surrender and of truce.
Consistently therefore it is appropriately the flag of victory and of war.
It is thus seen that an attribute of a color which ordinarily may be unpopular, can be used to advantage in a proper place.
The physiological or psychological effect of this unsatisfactory colordifference is unknown but that it is annoying is the testimony of many observers.
Therefore, the color white was often employed to denote nobility and virtue.
Hence, society equates white with virtue and black with vice, perpetuating hierarchies.
passage: White naturally assumed the attributes of chastity, virginity, purity, innocence, fidelity, peace and friendship; and mythology and poetry abound with examples of such usage. It also symbolizes weakness perhaps through association with femininity and was the mark of untried manhood. Consistently therefore it is appropriately the flag of surrender and of truce. Black naturally is quite opposite in character to white, being characterized as dismal, desolate, deadly, malignant, threatening, evil, chaotic and loathesome.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
There are many factors such as brightness-contrast and the position of the units which determine the limiting brightness so that it is impossible to be very definite without discussing these factors at great length. Specifications of brightness apply to the unit as installed and equipped with light-sources whereas the direct-indirect classification has the advantage of definiteness regarding the distribution of light obtainable from the unit. Such a classification or specification of lighting units in terms of direct and indirect components appears to satisfy certain practical requirements of definite description of the characteristics of individual units but there is still a lack of definiteness in the description of lighting installations in general, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]. Cove-lighting in which the light-sources are concealed behind a projecting moulding or cornice would be correctly classified as indirect lighting and there appears to be no reason why the direct-indirect terminology could not be applied to the distribution of light by the lighting system as a whole as determined at any point of interest.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: unless such a classification be applied to the distribution of light as a whole
unless such a classification be applied to the distribution of light in parts

if a direct-indirect luminous bowl is a deep yellow in color most persons will unconsciously associate this color with the useful light even if most of the latter arrives indirectly at the point of interest practically unaltered by reflections from neutral walls and ceiling
unless one considers the variability in lighting conditions and observer perception
unless the installation's geometry, fixture arrangement, and surrounding architecture are fully specified in detail
passage: There are many factors such as brightness-contrast and the position of the units which determine the limiting brightness so that it is impossible to be very definite without discussing these factors at great length. Specifications of brightness apply to the unit as installed and equipped with light-sources whereas the direct-indirect classification has the advantage of definiteness regarding the distribution of light obtainable from the unit. Such a classification or specification of lighting units in terms of direct and indirect components appears to satisfy certain practical requirements of definite description of the characteristics of individual units but there is still a lack of definiteness in the description of lighting installations in general, unless such a classification be applied to the distribution of light as a whole. Cove-lighting in which the light-sources are concealed behind a projecting moulding or cornice would be correctly classified as indirect lighting and there appears to be no reason why the direct-indirect terminology could not be applied to the distribution of light by the lighting system as a whole as determined at any point of interest.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
A few extra outlets for portable lamps, simple combinations of lighting principles in portable lamps and in suspended fixtures, two- and three-circuit fixtures each providing light of different distribution and possibly of different tint are possibilities for obtaining a distribution and color of light to suit the mood or the occasion. However, the provisions for obtaining variety in lighting seldom extend beyond a twocircuit unit providing only a possibility of varying the intensity of light, and a few connections for portable lamps, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. Of all lighting systems, Nature's lighting is the least monotonous and a study of it leads to the conclusion that this is due.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: although simple combinations of accessories and of multiple circuits are sources of this most appealing feature of lighting
although even complex arrangements of accessories and single circuits lack this most appealing feature of lighting
although this is often annoying, the lack of monotony is one of the attractive features of daylight
although no positive proof is available, there is a possibility that much of the complaint against artificial lighting may be due to insufficient intensities or quantities of light
although nature provides an array of varying light levels and colors throughout the day
though some designers incorporate advanced dimming systems to achieve nuanced color shifts and mood lighting throughout the space
passage: A few extra outlets for portable lamps, simple combinations of lighting principles in portable lamps and in suspended fixtures, two- and three-circuit fixtures each providing light of different distribution and possibly of different tint are possibilities for obtaining a distribution and color of light to suit the mood or the occasion. However, the provisions for obtaining variety in lighting seldom extend beyond a twocircuit unit providing only a possibility of varying the intensity of light, and a few connections for portable lamps, although simple combinations of accessories and of multiple circuits are sources of this most appealing feature of lighting. Of all lighting systems, Nature's lighting is the least monotonous and a study of it leads to the conclusion that this is due.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities, a technical handbook published in 1917 by Matthew Luckiesh, an American physicist.
main_question:
In order to further exemplify the importance of visualization in lighting and what is to be visualized let us inquire further into the process of seeing. The analysis of human consciousness reveals two kinds of visual sensations, namely, chromatic and achromatic sensations. No masterpiece of painted or sculptured art, no beautiful landscape, no office or factory operation and no lighting effect, [masked clause describing an inference or effect].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The Lighting Art, its Practice and Possibilities
answer_strings: so far as the visual sense is concerned, consists of more than an arrangement or sequence of varied colors and brightnesses
so far as the visual sense is concerned, consists of less than an arrangement or sequence of monotonous hues and dullness
so the most practicable terminology will provide terms which will give an idea of the appearance of colors
so that, owing to all these influences daylight is far from being constant
can be fully appreciated so long as it is properly illuminated
then the viewer's perception is fundamentally altered, revealing hidden emotional layers and subtle spatial nuances previously unnoticed in the scene
passage: In order to further exemplify the importance of visualization in lighting and what is to be visualized let us inquire further into the process of seeing. The analysis of human consciousness reveals two kinds of visual sensations, namely, chromatic and achromatic sensations. No masterpiece of painted or sculptured art, no beautiful landscape, no office or factory operation and no lighting effect, so far as the visual sense is concerned, consists of more than an arrangement or sequence of varied colors and brightnesses.
